digest: Standard-depth handoff: most core controls for safely running AI coding agents locally/CI are verified; vendor-specific coverage and incident/small-team synthesis remain caveated.

finalReport.summary: Working conclusion: adopt a default-deny, least-privilege operating model for AI coding agents. Run local agent work in a sandbox/container/worktree with narrow filesystem mounts, deny network egress unless needed, keep secrets out of agent-reachable environments, require human review for code/dependency/CI/deploy changes, and retain PR/CI/audit evidence for incident response. This is strongly supported for general controls, GitHub Actions, GitLab runner risk, Docker no-network isolation, npm/pip install risks, OWASP prompt-injection defenses, Claude Code settings, and GitHub Copilot firewall/environment behavior. Treat OpenAI Codex, Gemini, small-team cost claims, and full incident-playbook details as caveated unless verified against current primary docs.

researchMetadata.sourcePolicy: Primary vendor/platform/security docs preferred; audit-claims verdicts are authoritative; exact quantitative or vendor-specific claims are not promoted without URL-backed support.

factSlotCoverage.bestValue: Strong primary evidence for Anthropic Claude Code and GitHub Copilot; OpenAI Codex exact details improved but remain too limited for broad OpenAI-specific hardening; Gemini not covered.
factSlotCoverage.parentImpact: High: vendor-specific recommendations must name covered vendors and caveat missing ones.

factSlotCoverage.bestValue: Use sandbox/container/worktree boundaries; avoid broad host/home mounts; mounted workspace files remain accessible inside devcontainers.
Default-deny local egress with Docker --network none where possible; treat CI runner networks as RCE infrastructure; Copilot firewall is useful but not comprehensive.
Use least-privilege CI credentials, OIDC instead of long-lived cloud secrets, avoid exposing local/CI secrets to agent code, and rotate secrets if exposed.
factSlotCoverage.parentImpact: Critical: credential exposure is highest-impact failure mode.

factSlotCoverage.bestValue: Treat repo/issues/logs/web/tool output as untrusted data; separate instructions from data; constrain tools with validation, permissions, least privilege, monitoring, and HITL for high-risk actions.
npm lifecycle scripts execute package-controlled code; ignore-scripts mitigates install scripts with caveats; pip sources and hash requirements should be controlled; dependency changes need review.
factSlotCoverage.evidenceStatus: verified for npm/pip, partial for broader frameworks

factSlotCoverage.bestValue: Retain PR/commit/review records, CI logs, deployment approvals, and org audit-log actor/action/time exports; log LLM/tool interactions where available.
factSlotCoverage.parentImpact: Medium: enables accountability and incident response.

factSlotCoverage.bestValue: Increase approvals with risk: tool permission rules locally, PR review for code changes, environment/deployment reviews for secret/deploy actions, HITL for high-risk LLM actions.
Pre-plan incident handling, log/audit review, token revocation/rotation, workflow disablement, rollback, recovery, and post-incident review; some elements need stronger primary sourcing.
factSlotCoverage.parentImpact: High: final checklist can include a practical but caveated playbook.

factSlotCoverage.bestValue: Feasible baseline appears to be PR review, denied secrets paths, allowlisted routine commands, CODEOWNERS/protected environments, and audit-log retention; this is synthesized from built-in controls, not measured cost research.
factSlotCoverage.parentImpact: Medium: phrase as phased implementation, not proven cost model.

factSlotCoverage.bestValue: OWASP documents prompt-injection impacts; optional incident examples were not verified enough for headline use.
factSlotCoverage.parentImpact: Low: useful for narrative, not needed for implementation checklist.

factSlotCoverage.bestValue: CI jobs are untrusted code execution with access to configured tokens/secrets; use least privilege, avoid dangerous PR triggers, and do not run untrusted jobs on shell/privileged runners.

mainFindings.finding: Network and secret exposure are the highest-risk paths; combine egress denial/allowlists with least-privilege credentials and OIDC where supported.
Prompt injection is not just chat input: repository content, issues, web pages, tool outputs, and similar context must be treated as untrusted data and separated from trusted instructions.
sources: OWASP LLM Prompt Injection Cheat Sheet

mainFindings.finding: Local isolation should use explicit sandbox/container boundaries and narrow mounts; devcontainers isolate runtimes but still expose mounted workspace files.
CI runners should be treated as remote-code-execution infrastructure; shell executors and privileged Docker runners are inappropriate for untrusted/agent-generated jobs.

Dependency installs are executable supply-chain events: npm lifecycle scripts can run during install/ci, and pip can fetch from indexes, VCS, local directories, or archives; use flags and review gates accordingly.
Auditability can be built from existing platform trails: PRs, commits, deployment reviews, CI logs, and GitHub org audit-log exports.

recommendations.recommendation: Adopt a tiered autonomy policy: read-only agents may inspect code; write-capable agents require sandboxed workspace and PR review; shell/network/CI/deploy actions require explicit allowlists and human approval.
evidence: OWASP HITL and tool permission guidance
recommendations.caveat: Do not encode unsupported vendor-specific rules for Gemini or over-specific Copilot one-PR claims.

recommendations.recommendation: For local runs, use a disposable worktree/container/devcontainer with no broad home or secrets mounts; use Docker --network none or equivalent when internet is not required.

For CI, separate untrusted agent-produced code from privileged jobs, use least-privilege tokens, avoid long-lived cloud secrets via OIDC where available, and gate secret/deploy environments with deployment reviews.

Treat dependency and lockfile changes as high-risk: run install commands in isolated environments, disable package install scripts where compatible, use hash/lock controls for Python where feasible, and require human review for package manager changes.
recommendations.caveat: Broader OpenSSF/SLSA/CISA supply-chain guidance was not fully harvested; cite only npm/pip docs unless follow-up is done.

recommendations.recommendation: Retain evidence for agent actions: prompt/task transcript where available, tool-call logs, git diff/commit/PR review records, CI logs, deployment approvals, and audit-log exports.
recommendations.caveat: Full AI-agent-specific incident evidence-capture guidance remains partially supported.

actionPlan.step: Inventory agent modes and classify each by capabilities: read, write, shell, network, dependency install, CI edit, secret access, deploy.
Implement local baseline: disposable branch/worktree, constrained mounts, deny home/secrets paths, default-deny network, and explicit command allowlist.
Implement CI baseline: least-privilege GITHUB_TOKEN or equivalent, OIDC for cloud access where supported, no secrets on untrusted PR/agent jobs, protected environments for deploy/secret jobs.
Add dependency-change gate: require reviewer approval for package manifests, lockfiles, install-script enabling, VCS/archive dependency sources, and CI workflow changes.
Define incident runbook: pause agent credentials/workflows, preserve logs/PRs/diffs, rotate exposed secrets, audit dependency/action changes, roll back bad changes, and conduct post-incident review.
actionPlan.caveat: Use as a starting runbook; verify full containment/recovery/postmortem requirements against the organization’s IR standard.

caveatedFindings.finding: GitHub Copilot cloud agent is supported as running in its own ephemeral GitHub Actions-powered environment and making changes on a branch before a PR, but strict repository/branch/one-PR-per-task scoping was over-specific.
An AI-agent compromise playbook is sensible, and NIST/GitHub support planned incident handling, logs, and secret rotation, but inspected evidence did not directly cover every listed playbook element.
Small-team feasibility is an inference from built-in controls, not a measured cost or productivity study.

unverifiedButRelevant.item: Google Gemini CLI/Code Assist sandboxing and approval controls were not covered by verified primary evidence.
unverifiedButRelevant.whyRelevant: Would affect vendor-specific checklist language if the parent uses Gemini.

unverifiedButRelevant.item: Broader supply-chain frameworks such as SLSA/OpenSSF/CISA were not fully harvested.
unverifiedButRelevant.whyRelevant: Would strengthen enterprise dependency-change policy beyond npm/pip mechanics.

unverifiedButRelevant.item: Known coding-agent prompt-injection/exfiltration case studies were preserved as optional but not verified for final claims.
unverifiedButRelevant.whyRelevant: Could improve risk communication but should not drive hard controls without verification.

unverifiedButRelevant.item: GitLab CI secrets/external-secrets exact behavior was not core-selected, though runner-security evidence is strong.

parentDecisionNotes.note: Decide which AI coding-agent vendors are in scope before publishing vendor-specific controls.
parentDecisionNotes.whyItMatters: Claude and GitHub Copilot are well supported; OpenAI Codex is partially refreshed; Gemini is absent.
parentDecisionNotes.suggestedParentDecision: Use vendor-neutral controls as default and add vendor appendices only after primary-doc verification.

parentDecisionNotes.note: Choose whether completeness or strict verification matters more for the incident runbook.
parentDecisionNotes.whyItMatters: A practical runbook is needed, but some elements are supported by general IR principles rather than AI-agent-specific primary evidence.
parentDecisionNotes.suggestedParentDecision: Publish a caveated baseline runbook now, then validate against internal IR policy/NIST full text.

parentDecisionNotes.note: Avoid numeric thresholds or hard cost claims for small teams.
parentDecisionNotes.whyItMatters: No measured small-team feasibility study was verified.
parentDecisionNotes.suggestedParentDecision: Frame as phased adoption using built-in platform controls, not as a guaranteed lightweight program.

parentDecisionNotes.note: Do not rely on Copilot firewall as the only egress control.
parentDecisionNotes.whyItMatters: GitHub docs support the firewall being enabled/recommended but not comprehensive.
parentDecisionNotes.suggestedParentDecision: Layer firewall controls with secret separation, review gates, and least-privilege CI.

notCovered: Google Gemini CLI/Code Assist primary controls

blocking.gap: Gemini and some OpenAI Codex vendor-specific controls are not fully verified.
blocking.whyItMatters: Blocks precise vendor comparison or vendor-specific hardening defaults.
blocking.wouldChange: Could add or alter sandbox/approval/network settings for those tools.

nonBlockingCaveats.gap: Supply-chain standards beyond npm/pip were not fully harvested.
nonBlockingCaveats.whyItMatters: Limits broader policy framing but not core package-manager mitigations.
nonBlockingCaveats.wouldChange: Could strengthen dependency-review recommendations with standard terminology.

nonBlockingCaveats.gap: Small-team feasibility is inferred, not empirically measured.
nonBlockingCaveats.wouldChange: Could justify a stricter or more phased rollout.

nonBlockingCaveats.gap: Incident playbook elements are partially supported.
nonBlockingCaveats.whyItMatters: A runbook is useful but should align with internal IR policy and stronger sources.
Affects narrative examples, not baseline controls.
nonBlockingCaveats.wouldChange: Could improve training material and stakeholder buy-in.

claims.supportSummary: Claude Code settings support sandbox filesystem/network rules and sandboxed Bash auto-approval.
claims.caveat: Network evidence came from sandbox examples/restrictions.

claims.supportSummary: Copilot cloud agent uses an ephemeral GitHub Actions-powered environment and can make branch/PR changes.
claims.caveat: Strict repository/branch/one-PR-per-task scoping was not established.
claims.correctionOrCounterclaim: Use narrower wording: ephemeral environment and branch/PR workflow.

claims.supportSummary: Copilot firewall/recommended allowlist is default but not comprehensive.
claims.caveat: Page naming shifted to cloud agent.

claims.supportSummary: Primary OpenAI docs define approval_policy and sandbox_mode details relevant to Codex recommendations.
claims.caveat: Some snippets were term-matched; avoid over-specific OpenAI guidance without fresh extraction.

claims.supportSummary: Dev Containers separate runtimes/tools but mounted/copied/cloned workspace files remain accessible.
Docker --network none isolates container network except loopback.
claims.caveat: Default-deny egress is an operational inference.

claims.supportSummary: CI tokens/secrets available to jobs/actions require least privilege and separation from untrusted code.
claims.caveat: Docs discuss untrusted code generally, not AI-generated code specifically.

claims.supportSummary: GitHub recommends OIDC to avoid long-lived cloud secrets.
claims.caveat: Applies when cloud provider supports OIDC.

claims.supportSummary: OWASP supports treating external/repo/web content as untrusted data separated from instructions.
claims.caveat: Logs are supported by broader principle more than named snippet.

claims.supportSummary: OWASP supports permission checks, session context, parameter validation, least privilege, and HITL for high-risk actions.
npm install/ci lifecycle scripts can run; ignore-scripts has explicit-script caveats.
claims.caveat: Cited npm docs are v10 pages.

claims.supportSummary: pip supports multiple source types and --require-hashes for repeatable hash-checked installs.
claims.caveat: Current stable docs, not pinned pip version.

claims.supportSummary: GitLab treats pipelines as remote code execution; shell and privileged Docker runners are unsafe for untrusted jobs.
claims.caveat: Agent-generated jobs are inferred as untrusted jobs.

claims.supportSummary: GitHub environments/deployment reviews can gate jobs and environment secrets.
claims.caveat: Availability varies by repository visibility and plan.

claims.supportSummary: GitHub org audit logs record actor/action/time and export JSON/CSV.
claims.caveat: Evidence retention is inferred from export support.

claims.supportSummary: NIST/GitHub support planned incident handling, logs, and secret rotation.
claims.caveat: Full containment/evidence/recovery/post-review list was not directly established.
claims.correctionOrCounterclaim: Use narrower wording until full IR sources are verified.
